description: Apollo 12 Flight Journal - Day 4, part 3: Lunar Orbit Revolutions 1 and 2

tokens: Well, Houston, I was just looking at one very, very, odd, and it looks like a very fresh impact crater that is sort of collapsed on one side -that we've been looking at and discussing a little bit.
It's got some fairly high raised sides on it, and we haven't quite got ourselves oriented to the proper size yet.
I think these craters are much bigger than anything we've ever seen on Earth, so we're - We're just sitting here discussing various sizes and getting ourselves oriented on the map.
I just broke out the monocular, and we're starting to look with it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: One interesting thing, Paul, was that in some of these large craters - the old ones, it looks like - as you look towards the distance, you can see that they - at least from here - appear darker, and it looks very flat like the mare looked from Earth, but the same size ones that you pass right over, they don't look a bit darker than any other of the terrain.
So I guess it must have something to do with the way the Sun refracts off the - the surface.
We're passing a beautiful impact crater here on our right side now.
It's got many, many long rays; it's a beauty.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That was Al Bean commenting on the lunar surface below them.
The spacecraft currently 87.1 nautical miles above the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: It's - This impact crater that we're going over right now, which has such a fantastic ejecta pattern that we can see it so well, the ejecta pattern's got to go out 50 or 60 crater diameters, very easily discernible with the eye.
And you can almost - You can pretty well tell the direction of the impact from looking at the ejecta blanket, but it - it's rea1ly spectacular.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We'll be coming up with the TV in just a few minutes.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We can't quite see that yet, but we've been looking over at Humboldt and looking at all the great fracture marks in it and everything.
Actually, it looks to me like some criss-cross roads down there in the desert or something.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're starting to get a television picture now from the spacecraft."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're starting to get a picture now.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: For information, you should be crossing 60-degrees east at 84:02:08.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: What do you see on the tube, Houston?
I can't tell too much from the monitor.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're getting the - the lunar surface.
It appears that we can see the subsolar point, or could at one time there.
We can see numerous small light-colored craters.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I'm going to hand it over to Dick.
He's got Langrenus out the window.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're coming up on Vox to you, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: He's pointing it over toward Langrenus now.
This Petavius over here is a beautiful thing.
Whenever you get finished, I'll see if I can shoot it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: It's got high peaks in the middle.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: [Garble] there's the central peaks right in Langrenus right there...
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: I can see those pretty well.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: On the screen, the colors appear to be green to brown.
Can you describe the colors in the scene that you're seeing there now?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: The - mountain that's sort of [garble] sort of in the center of Langrenus I'm looking at through the monocular, and apparently, they look very smooth with the naked eye, but by looking through the monocular, I can pick up these black dots.
They're very black, and I - they're obviously very large boulders sitting that are around on it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The picture on the TV screen...
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: ...of the central peak - it looked kind of -it appeared to be rather rounded.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: It appears to be rounded, but it's got a lot of big boulders sitting on it.
I'll hand the camera over to Al, now.
He's got some stuff out his window.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Oops, that's one for the geologists, the materials out his window he wants to shine it down on.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You were asking what color it looks like from here.
It's changed slightly as we went around it.
At first, it had a very, very light gray-white concrete appearance.
Now, it has more of a - it's still a light-gray concrete, but it has a little - just a touch of brown in it.
At least, that's the way it appears to me.
That's the - the Moon that's directly below us.
Of course, there's several places that are very, very white, and I'll point the camera at one of them now.
It's a small crater, and it's very symmetrical.
It just looks like a cone with a flat bottom.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: It's going out the top of the screen.
Can you move up and pick it up just a little?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: ...crater appears to be white down inside, Al.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: It's white, and then it's got some radial streaks of a more darker material.
It's rolling down, or at least runs from the rim down to the center - down to the flat bottom.
I think you can see those on the TV.
Let me show you a real bright crater that's more towards the horizon, but it's one of the very, very bright ones.
I'll try to put it right in the center.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I can see one that's just a little bit above the edge of the window there.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Another interesting thing is - This white or gray-white Moon, it contrasts very starkly with the black sky just like everyone's reported.
And maybe even so on the TV down there.
But the black is about as black as you've ever seen in your life.
It's just - doesn't have any - any hues or anything to it.
It's just solid straight dull black, and then the Moon is just sort of very light concrete color.
In fact, if I wanted to look at something that I thought was about the same color as the Moon, I'd go out and look at my driveway.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Not near as - even Earth orbit - Even Earth orbit at night or the daytime, the sky was never as black as it is here.
This is the blackest black I ever saw.
Al described it as dull, and it doesn't even seem like a dull black when you look at it on the horizon, to me.
It's as coal black as I've ever seen.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I'm going to pass the TV over to Dick now.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Show you - There's a very interesting crater that Dick's got down there.
There's another one that I'm looking into; it's the first one I've seen with the fractures in the bottom of it that we've flown right directly over.

And there's a fracture pattern that runs right through the middle of the crater including the rim of it, perpendicular to it, crosses all the way across the crater, so it gives me the feeling that - that the fracture pattern - that particular fracture doesn't have anything to do with the crater.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: What are looking at there now, 12?
Is that the bottom of the crater or a Mare area?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: That's at the bottom of a crater, a large Mare area actually, Paul.
And in the crater, there are two - in the Maria, there are two brand new craters that have fairly detailed ray patterns I going out from them.
They're quite start - quite I startling when you see them, because they are I perfect radial patterns from two of them, right next to each other.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: As a matter of fact, that one ray pattern looks like it's only one direction.
It look that way to you, Dick?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: It looks like it's pointing towards us.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: That double-ray pattern is just a single one.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: That is a beautiful crater over here to the right.
It's not particularly spectacular, but it's pretty symmetrical, and it's got some interesting sloping in on the sides.
It looks like an old, old one.
We're getting ready to pass over the Sea of Fertility right now.
I guess we are over it.
There's a lot of rilles and some cracks in here we ought to be able to pick up on the TV.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're passing over the Sea of Fertility now, and it is a little bit darker than the terra that we've been over, but not so much.
It's more of a - just a slightly darker gray.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Looks like the beach sand down at Galveston when-ever it's wet.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We had a team of geologists checking your driveway.
We'll send them to Galveston now.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Looking down into a real fresh impact crater in the Sea of Fertility; and, with the monocular, I can see some pretty large boulders.
So I guess, as high as we are, if I can see those boulders, they must be pretty darn big.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We can see that crater in the lower left part of the screen right now.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Yes, let me show this impact crater or you got something you want to show them, Pete?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: You can sure see the direction that one came in from, can't you, Pete?
There's hardly any ejecta to our right, which would be - to the south, I guess.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That one you're looking into now, Houston, that's the one I was saying.
It's really got some big boulders in the bottom of it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You're breaking up a little on Vox, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We see that crater there which has a ray pattern through about everything but about a 110-120- degrees of it off to the right.
Is that the one to which you're referring?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Understand that you can see larger boulders in the bottom of the crater?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Yes, and also along the sides of it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Hey, there's a big one we have right there now.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Yes, that one just came into view.
I guess that's still in the Sea of Fertility.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're getting ready to pass over Theophilus here, Pete.
It's going to be right down at your lower 1 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That one we were showing you a moment ago doesn't have a name, and it's - it's interesting just to the - behind that small one you were showing is sort of a whole streak of light-colored material that runs for hundreds of miles there and it's a different shape.
Maybe it's just the elevation in that area; I don't know.
It's near the central peak, so I'll use the monocular on it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We got them good in the screen, and we saw that large white ray as you switched windows there.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: It's kind of interesting if you look right on - one thing about the peaks, I'm looking at them through the monocular; and, on the top of the peaks, you can see a great number of what appear to be boulders, and they must be huge to be visible from the altitude we're at right now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Understand there are boulders on the tops of the peaks, and they look like some fairly well defined ridge lines in that central peak.
Is that how they look through the monocular?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: That TV must be coming through real well, because from here it looks like very sharp ridge lines; and, if you look at either side of the ridge line, you can see sort of a terracing, a mild terracing effect that's parallel to the ridge line.
It could be some form of something or - in fact, some sort of transportational mechanism there, I don't know what it is.
Obviously, you can see some - what would be termed rilles and [garble] running down from the ridge line.
They're running perpendicular to it, more or less.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You ought to be able to look over to your side real far over there...
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: ...and see the Sea of Serenity and some of those craters over in there.
They ought to be pretty good contrast where that dark bar is.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Yes, that ought - that might be interesting.
Hey, there's some pretty good cracks over there.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Hey, look at that rille over there, Dick.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We're going to change windows here.
There's some beautiful rilles over in the other side, north side.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I don't know, maybe we ought to try this one.
Can you see them out of that one better?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: There's some grabens over here, big long ones that come down the [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: There's a very sharp crater you can see in the top left part of the screen right now.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: In the view, we can see now, there appear to be a dark line running from the lower left up toward the top center.
Can you make anything out of that?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: That's what I'm trying to show you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Looks like some fair looks like some fairly deep rilles and droppings over there, particularly the ones that you ask about.
There's a nice wide one over there, Pete.
Over by that fresh impact with the raised rim?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Maybe you'd better take the camera in the other window.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Put if over there with Dick.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: In that scene you just shifted from, there appeared to be two parallel rilles.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: As a matter of fact, in looking at it, there are two parallel rilles and then they actually pick up, well not quite a third one.
It's like one ends on one side, and the middle one goes all the way through, and then one picks up on the right-hand side.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Well, there's two parallel grabens in this sea.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Also - also on that - that rille - one of the last things we saw and, Hous- or at the Cape when we talked with the geologist was the little experiment that the guy did blowing air through the sand, gases that - Sure enough I've got some examples of that right here in those trench-like structures.
There's some crater chains running through them and alongside of them, just like we talked about...
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: There's a big giant up here just blowing sand all over the place.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Now, we can see - just a minute ago a - furrow or trench coming from the lower left side of the screen up toward the center and kind of ending in a string of rather poorly defined craters.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: There's one up here that's actually a double - double graben; it's not offset.
There are two of them running parallel to each other.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: You see the double craters right there?
Do you see the grabens running alongside of them?
It looks like a string of craters.
Can you give them that on the TV?
I may get it out of this window.
Let me try the center window.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: While you are setting up, how - how's the view out of your windows now?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Window number 5 is a good one, window number 4 is poor, window number 3, which is the hatch window, it's not -it's bad - it's still the same condition it was at launch.
But, because we've got a bright background instead of the dark background we had before, the marks on them aren't quite as noticeable.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're getting very good pictures here.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Number 1 is by far the worst window.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: What you're seeing is - a rille here with a whole bunch of - looks like vent holes running along these rilles.
Can you see those from the ground, Paul?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We could see them before - You just moved off of what appears to be a rill moving to the right now.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: [Garble] over on your side there, Dick.

There's the - there's several - there's a whole bunch of areas in here, just looking out the window generally, that give you the feeling that, as we've talked about with the geologists, that some of this is volcanic action in here.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Now, we can see a crater just to the left of the screen there that's rather poorly defined and appears to be a good-sized rille or fracture running across the floor.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Hey, we've got the straight wall coming up on this side, Houston.
When it gets a little bit closer, I'll show it to you.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Also, we've got a couple - here; let me borrow that thing, Dick, a minute.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Kind of ridiculous not to be taking pictures.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Is the camera over there, Al?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Now, I don't know where he put it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Can you see the straight wall now, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We can see it very good.
Just beyond that large crater was another smaller crater and its rim, and we can see the wall on beyond it.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: You got this little chart right here.
We've been - since the update, we've been here 56 minutes, so leave it 56 minutes, Data.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: I'm going to move off the straight wall, now, Houston, and look down at a crater.
I don't have my chart here, so I don't know the name of it, but it's got extremely well defined terraced walls.
It's got a nice central peak in the middle.
And let me see if I can get it better for you.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We have a good one there, Al.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: And it also has a nice crack right down at the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That's a pretty impressive looking crater.
We're in the terra now, and you can tell that the ground is much more hummocky and quite a bit rougher than the Mare which we're getting ready to approach in a few moments.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Here's another one with a good central peak.
In fact, this is one of the highest central peaks that - that's a hard one to show because the radar antenna of the LM partially eclipsing it.
Can you see that central peak there?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: It's almost as high as the rim itself.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Sun's getting a lot lower now, as we approach the terminator...
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Let me point the camera back past the straight wall again.
Let me give you another view of that, because it's coming into even greater relief from this angle.
Then, I'll go back and give you the horizon, because that's one of the most impressive things right here.
Take a look at those mountains.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I'll move a little bit around and see if I can - here's some of those mountains we talked about earlier that look like, from the distance, like little clouds over the Mare.
You can see how bright they are relative to the Mare surface; and, maybe even on the TV, they look like puffy clouds.
They look like hard rock down there.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Got something out your window, Pete?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: There's a high mountain right there on the horizon.
I bet you - do you see that very high mountain on the horizon, Houston?
It's about the center of the screen now.
All you can see is reflected light.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Can you open your f-stops any - get in this dim light?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Here's an interesting feature down there that you're looking at near that large crater.
The hole is - seems to be a general trending of ridges in this area, all in the same direction.
They'd be running, I guess, on your camera from the top right-hand corner to the lower left.
And it's particularly evident down there by the large crater that's in your picture.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: There it is right - you see it?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: There's - I guess it's probably parallel to - let me show you some more of these clouds.
They just seem to be large bumps on top of the Mare.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Hey, let me show them Mosting over here, Al.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Schroter's Valley's up to the north.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: You can open it again, if you want it.
Boy, it's beautiful right in - look at that crater.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Straight wall pretty impressive in this light.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Now, it's starting to get a little dark outside.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, whenever you get finished - whenever you get finished, there's a beauty over here, Dick.
Whenever you get finished, there's a beauty over here.
We're getting to where we get that low Sun angle; we can see those little craters within the big ones.
I don't - I think they're probably in all of them; we just can't see them.
There's the Sun in this one.
It looks like it's somebody hit it with a bunch of buckshot right...
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I wonder which - I wonder which one of those shadows down there is a 5-degree Sun Angle?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You just passed over 5-degree Sun angle on the surface.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Okay, that's what I was just trying to look at here and see what I could see.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Yes, there's some beauties over here.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We got a beauty for you, Houston.
See if I can run up the f-stop for you.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Okay, that's coming in good, Al.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Take a look at - down in that crater, the number of other small craters.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let me - soon as you look at that one, I'll show you the horizon; and near the terminator on the hori - distant horizon, you can easily see the curvature of the Moon, and you can also see the stark contrast between the horizon and the bright rocks and the dark crater.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Hey Paul, all I can say, it's another fighter pilot's life: Hours and hours.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: That crater's a beauty out there.
The rim is illuminated by the low Sun while, down inside the crater it's dark.
And you can see the ray patterns from here, and you can see the mare surface.
It almost looks like somebody took a - some cake icing and spread it with a big knife, laid it all around out there, and then somebody shot some BB's in it.
It's got that layering all over it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Mosting, and that [garble] up Schroter's Valley.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let me hand it back to you.
Now the [garble] down in the terminator.
You ought to have Copernicus out there somewhere.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: You should see all that stuff that's on the window.
That's why that window isn't any good for photography out of it.
The Sun's really shining right on it now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We can see all that stuff, Pete.
However, we're getting a good picture through it in the open spaces.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I guess Copernicus is too far out there to see, isn't it, Dick?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Guess we passed it a minute ago.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: That silly thing floating outside the window.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Now where'd that little piece of blue and gold stuff - whatever it is.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Well, Houston, that looks like that's about it for this pass.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You sure can't see anything on the other side of the terminator.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: For information, all spacecraft systems are in excellent shape.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: An interpretation of the map update follows:MAP UPDATE REV:second orbit around the MoonLOS:85:08:42 - Loss of Signal180°:85:31:16 - the time the CSM crosses the 180th meridian on the far side of the Moon, signifying the start of a new orbit.AOS:85:52:27 - Acquisition of Signal by MSFN
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On the ground, we're seeing some changes in signal strength, both up and down.
Have you changed any of your communications modes recently?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: You mean over the last few minutes or what?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The only thing we've done is turn the TV off.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: All the other Comm switches appear to be in the normal position, and the only thing we did was turn TV off.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Your Comm to us has been super.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Five minutes to LOS; we'll see you in 49 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We're settled down to a nice meal, and we're allowing ourselves a little music on the tape recorder.
And we'll see you on the other side.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, who won the vote on what you're playing on the tape recorder?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We play a little bit of Al's, and a little bit of Dick's, and a little bit of mine.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 85 hours, 7 minutes.
Flight Director Glynn Lunney, now going around the room getting a final status before Loss Of Signal.
We will be loosing contact with Apollo 12 in 1 minute, 40 seconds, reacquiring again at 85 hours, 52 minutes, 26 seconds.
We are now 1 minute from Loss Of Signal, everything looking good as the spacecraft goes around the corner, we will be reacquiring in little less than 44 minutes.
We have had Loss Of Signal now, acquisition to occur in 43 minutes, 24 seconds.
Apollo 12 currently in an orbit 168.7 by 62.5.
The orbital period is 2 hours, 8 minutes, 48 seconds.
We are reading an orbital weight of 71,983 pounds at the present time.
As we lost acquisition with the spacecraft, all systems looking very good.
At 85 hours, 10 minutes, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: In an attempt to troubleshoot this variation in signal strength which we experienced for a little bit after the TV pass, will you verify the position that you had the High-Gain Antenna Track switch in at that time, please?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Roger; and it's still in Reacq, Narrow.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay, understand, and I've got an update to your CSM alternate and contingency checklist, if you want to break it out.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Okay, give you a call in a second.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Are you going to give me [garble]?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay, in column Charlie, line 7, the number was formerly 27340; change that to read 22434.
This puts in the new Delta-H from your P23s
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We've all ready got it in.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Be advised we dumped waste water to 10 percent and purged the fuel cells.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I've got your map update for Rev 3.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: An interpretation of the map update follows:MAP UPDATE REV:third orbit around the MoonLOS:87:17:09 - Loss of Signal180°:87:39:40 - the time the CSM crosses the 180th meridian on the far side of the Moon, signifying the start of a new orbit.AOS:88:01:22 - Acquisition of Signal by MSFN
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: If you'll give us P00 and Accept, we'll give you a state vector and a target load.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 86 hours, 12 minutes.
During the replay of the video tapes from the television transmission we reacquired Apollo 12 on schedule.
We've been in contact with the spacecraft now for about 19 minutes.
We'll pick up the tape recording we have of the conversation about 3 minutes up to now and then continue to standby for live conversations.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I have two PADs for you when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Your ullage will be two jets for 19 seconds.
Your sextant star will be occulted by the Moon until 87 hours.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Would you just give me the Noun 47 again?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: A full interpretation of the PAD follows:Purpose:This is a burn that shapes their lunar orbit to a more appropriate one for their orbital observations and the upcoming lunar landing.Propulsion/guidance (PROP/GUID):The burn would be made using the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine at the rear of the Service Module, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM weight (Noun 47):38,627 pounds (17,520.9 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):+1.45,° and -0.66°.

These are the angles away from the longitudinal axis the SPS engine bell will be set to for the burn.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):87 hours, 48 minutes, 47.39 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):-139.2 x, (-42.3); +0000.1 y, (+0.03);-0089.5 z, (-27.3).
These velocity components are expressed with respect to the local vertical.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 360°; Pitch, 220°; Yaw, 360°.

The attitude figure is with respect to the IMU orientation at the time of the burn.HA, expected apocynthion of resulting orbit (Noun 44):66.2 nautical miles (122.6 km).HP, expected pericynthion of resulting orbit (Noun 44):54.1 nautical miles (100.2 km).Delta-VT:fps 165.5(50.4 m/s).
This is essentially a vector sum of the above velocities and as such represents the total velocity change.Burn duration or burn time:17 seconds.Delta-VC:0159.4 fps.
This is the fps value that will be entered into the EMS to give it backup control of the burn.
The value is smaller than Delta-VTbecause the EMS cannot compensate for the tail-off impulse generated by the engine after the cut-off command.

By entering a lower number, (and should the G&N system fail to shut the engine down) the burn would be stopped early enough by the EMS, that the extra thrust from the tail-off would bring the final Delta-V to the required value.Sextant star:Star 45 (Fomalhaut, Alpha Piscis Austrini) should be visible in the sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are 296.6° and 27.3° respectively.
This is one of the attitude checks the crew will make prior to the burn.Other notes include an ullage to settle the propellants in the SPS tanks, two RCS jets for 19 seconds before the burn.
The sextant star Fomalhaut won't be visible until after 87 hours GET due to being behind the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: LOI-2 SPS/G&N: 38627; plus 1.45, minus 0.66; 087:48:47.39; minus 0139.2, plus 0000.1, minus 0089.5; 360, 220, 360; 0066.2, plus 0054.1; 0165.5, 0:17, 0159.4; Fomalhaut, 45, 296.6, 27.3; two jets' ullage, 19 seconds; and the sextant star is occulted until 87:00.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's affirmative, and I have a TEl-5 PAD when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: The rest of the PAD is NA.
Your ullage is four jets for 11 seconds; that 's an undocked burn and assumes LOI-2.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I believe you said four jets, 11 seconds, assumes LOI-2.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's affirmative and that's an undocked burn, and your yaw trim for Noun 48 is plus 050.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Yaw trim plus 050, and it's undocked.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: A full interpretation of the PAD follows:Purpose:This is a burn that would send the crew back to Earth at the end of the fifth orbit around the Moon, should they need to make an emergency return.Propulsion/guidance (PROP/GUID):The burn would be made using the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine at the rear of the Service Module, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM weight (Noun 47):37,452 pounds (16,988 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.64° and +0.50°.

These are the angles away from the longitudinal axis the SPS engine bell will be set to for the burn.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):93 hours, 40 minutes, 32.35 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3,745.1 (+1,141.5); Y, +1,006.8 (+306.9); Z, -275.7 (-84.1).
These velocity components are expressed with respect to the local vertical.Spacecraft attitude:Roll - Not Applicable; Pitch, 81°; Yaw, - Not Applicable..

The attitude figure is with respect to the IMU orientation at the time of the burn.Other notes include a 4-jet, 11-second ullage to settle the SPS propellants in their tanks for the burn, will be CSM only, and presumes that the LOI-2 burn did not take place beforehand.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'll continue to standby live now for the rest of this front side pass.
Currently our displays here in Mission Control show the spacecraft at an altitude of 130.8 nautical miles.
Our current orbital figures are 169.5 by 61.6 and we show a combined weight of the CSM, LM in orbit of 72,212 pounds.

Included in the series of numbers read up to the crew were the numbers that they will use for the Lunar Orbit Insertion 2 maneuver, the burn which essentially circularizes the orbit changing the orbit from a 169.5 by 61.6 to a 54 by 66 nautical mile orbit and this orbit is targeted so that by the time the LM is ready to lift-off from the Moon and complete the rendezvous sequence with the orbiting Command Module it should be essentially circular at about 60 nautical miles.
The LOI-2 ignition is set for 87 hours, 48 minutes, 47 seconds.
The total Delta-V will be 161.6 feet per second, if total burn time of 17.07 seconds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Pete, throughout the flight, your oxygen consumption has been slightly higher than previous flights, by approximately 10 percent.
This is no problem, as extrapolating that to end of mission still gives us plenty of oxygen available.
In an attempt to try to figure out why, though, we have a couple of questions, primarily with respect to your use of the URA.
Approximately how long after use do you leave the cover valve in Vent before you close it?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We usually keep it on until the O2High light comes on, which is a couple of minutes worth of venting.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, and what position do you - do you leave the cover valve and the waste management over-board drain valve when you're not using the URA?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Usually shut the dump valve, Off.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We will massage that, thank you.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Okay, we probably have used a little more oxygen through that, and we noticed on our plot that we were running a little low on oxygen, also.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Are you looking at the DSKY?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: You're rolling into the High Gain limits.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: You can see quite well in Earth's shine, up here.

The LM is illuminated very brightly because it's fairly reflective, but the Moon itself is fairly easy to see in earthshine; it's quite beautiful, real soft, and sort of gives it a greenish tinge, gray-green.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And do you say that the LM is illuminated by earthshine?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: It's very reflective itself, and so it looks almost like the soft sunlight if there is such a thing.
But you can, for example, look out and read the marks on the Commander's overhead window; you can see all the Quads, the struts.
And real pretty up here in earthshine.
Kind of gives it sort of a gray-green cast, though.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 87 hours, 9 minutes.
We're now 8 minutes away from Loss Of Signal.
Here in Mission Control presently changing shifts.
Flight director Pete Frank will be replacing flight director Glynn Lunney.
The Capsule Communicator on this shift will continue to be astronaut Paul Weitz for the first part of the shift.
Don Lind taking over at about 4:30 this morning.
The Lunar Orbit Insertion number 2 maneuver scheduled to occur 39 minutes from now.
That burn is targeted for an Orbit of 54 by 66 nautical miles.
Time Of Ignition is tentatively set at 87 hours, 48 minutes, 47 seconds with a burn time of 17.07 seconds and a total Delta-V of 165.5 feet per second.
As we near the end of this front side pass all systems on the CSM continuing to look very good.
Flight director Glynn Lunney will be getting a final status from his flight controllers prior to Loss Of Signal and we expect passing along a goal for the Lunar Orbit Insertion number 2 maneuver.
At 87 hours, 11 minutes, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We just called up P40 for you to take a look at it before we disappear.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You're Go for LOI-2, and your PIPA's look real good.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Yes, it's been a long night here, too.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: No, it wasn't that; I had the water gun half in and half out, and I was trying to talk and drink at the same time.
Actually, we're - it's kind of interesting - Dick and Al and I have really switched over to this time schedule, and we're quite happy on it.
We - we're not really aware of the fact that it's the middle of the night back there.
transcript.speaker: Conrad
